attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of dyspnoea on exertion, fatigue, weight loss, and early satiety. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears pale. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His chest is clear to auscultation. His abdomen is soft with some fullness in the left upper quadrant and associated dullness to percussion.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 70 g/L (135-180)

  • Mean corpuscular volume: 86 fL (80-98)

  • White cell count: 5 x 10^9/L (4.00-11.00)

  • Platelets: 77 x 10^9/L (150-450)

  • Blood film: erythrocytes show marked anisopoikilocytosis with many teardrop cells and occasional nucleated red blood cells. Left-shifted leucocytosis with occasional blasts (2%) and myelocytes seen.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with myelofibrosis, a myeloproliferative neoplasm (MPN), which may be primary or secondary to polycythemia vera and essential thrombocythemia. Common clinical features are symptoms from anaemia, constitutional symptoms, and early satiety due to splenomegaly.

Common laboratory features are normocytic anaemia, leucocytosis, and thrombocytosis. The blood film typically shows dacrocytes (tear-drop cells), left-shifted granulopoiesis and nucleated red cells (leucoerythroblastic film). Bone marrow fibrosis leads to ineffective hematopoiesis and extramedullary hematopoiesis, typically in the spleen, causing progressive thrombocytopenia.

The pattern of abnormal red cell morphology (dacrocytes) is a feature of conditions that infiltrate the bone marrow. Apart from myelofibrosis, other conditions to consider include solid organ malignancies and storage disorders (e.g., Gaucher disease).

Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) is a mature B-lymphoproliferative disorder. While it can present with splenomegaly and cytopenia, the blood film findings in this case are not consistent with the diagnosis. In CLL, the blood film shows lymphocytosis with small mature lymphocytes, occasionally with features of haemolytic anaemia. Patients with CLL often have lymphadenopathy, which is not mentioned in this case.

Chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML), a MPN, usually presents with leukocytosis, often with a very high count. While CML can cause splenomegaly, the normal leucocyte count and the marked teardrop poikilocytosis make this diagnosis less likely than myelofibrosis.

Sideroblastic anaemia is characterised by the presence of ring sideroblasts in the bone marrow. While it can cause anaemia, it typically does not cause thrombocytopenia or splenomegaly. The presence of dacrocytes is also not a typical feature of sideroblastic anaemia.

Haemolytic anaemia can cause anaemia and occasionally splenomegaly, but it would not typically cause thrombocytopenia. Additionally, the blood film in haemolytic anaemia would show other features, such as spherocytes or schistocytes, rather than dacrocytes.
learningPoints: Myelofibrosis, a myeloproliferative neoplasm, may be primary or secondary to polycythemia vera and essential thrombocythemia.
Myelofibrosis typically presents with constitutional symptoms, symptoms from anaemia and splenomegaly.
In myelofibrosis, the blood film typically reveals teardrop poikilocytosis, nucleated red blood cells and left-shifted granulopoiesis (leucoerythroblastic film).
references: Tefferi A. Primary myelofibrosis: 2023 update on diagnosis, risk-stratification, and management. Am J Hematol. 2023;98(5):801-821. doi:10.1002/ajh.26857. Available at https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36680511/
